data.title: KONE (HLSE:KNEBV) Margin Decline Challenges Bullish Narratives on Profitability
data.description:
KONE Oyj (HLSE:KNEBV) posted a net profit margin of 8.7%, slightly down from 9% a year earlier, highlighting some pressure on profitability. Over the last five years, the company’s annual earnings growth has remained nearly flat at 0.01%. Looking ahead, investors are weighing the forecast for 10.4% annual earnings growth, which would be slower than the 16.2% expected for the Finnish market. Meanwhile, potential revenue growth of 5.3% per year gives KONE a slight edge over the broader market's 4%.

Margin Expansion Hinges on Modernization

  • Analysts anticipate profit margins rising from 8.7% today to 10.6% over the next three years, a key test for KONE’s shift toward high-margin modernization and recurring service revenue.
  • According to the analysts' consensus view, KONE’s focus on digital elevator upgrades and expanding its global service footprint is expected to deliver steadier, more predictable earnings.
    • Consensus highlights that these segments, reinforced by steady demand for energy-efficient upgrades and modernization, are likely to support margin growth even with patchy construction markets.
    • What stands out is that the push for innovative, connected elevator systems could offset risks from regions experiencing slow digital transformation. Recurring revenues from smart building solutions are expected to mature and bolster performance.
  • Curious if the shift to higher-margin services is really setting KONE apart? Bulls and bears are debating the outlook in the consensus narrative. See for yourself in the full write-up. 📊 Read the full KONE Oyj Consensus Narrative.

DCF Fair Value Signals Potential Upside

  • KONE trades at €58.66, but discounted cash flow analysis puts its fair value at €62.26 per share, hinting at possible undervaluation amid a high 31.1x price-to-earnings ratio.
  • According to the analysts' consensus view, the market appears to price KONE’s strong brand and quality of earnings above peer averages. However, with the current share price still below DCF fair value, some investors may see an opportunity.
    • Consensus narrative argues that high-quality past earnings and global diversification support this premium. Yet the near-equivalence of the current price to the analyst price target (€55.56) raises questions about how much near-term upside is left after recent gains.
    • Those focusing on discounted cash flow valuation will want to pay close attention to whether stronger growth in modernization and service segments materializes enough to close the value gap and eventually sustain a higher market multiple.

Dividend Sustainability Draws Investor Focus

  • The main risk flagged by analysts is the sustainability of KONE’s dividend, with ongoing investment in R&D and digital initiatives possibly putting long-term payouts under pressure if new innovations do not deliver incremental returns.
  • According to the analysts' consensus view, ongoing outlays for compliance and technology upgrades, especially in anticipation of stricter energy efficiency and sustainability regulations, could compress free cash flow and impact the company’s capacity to maintain or increase dividends.
    • Consensus notes that delays in KONE’s transition to more digitized, high-margin maintenance contracts or persistent weak demand in markets like China may also weigh on the company’s long-term ability to support dividends at current levels.
    • This mix of ambitious investment and exposure to volatile markets makes dividend reliability one of the biggest watchpoints for cautious investors.

data.title: AutoNation (AN) Margin Decline Reinforces Bearish Narratives Despite Discounted Valuation
data.description:
AutoNation (AN) delivered a 3% annual decline in earnings over the past five years, with net profit margins slipping to 2.3% from 2.9% last year and reporting negative earnings growth in the most recent period. While forward-looking forecasts point to 7.29% annual earnings growth, this rate trails the US market’s broader earnings growth of 15.5% per year and revenue growth expectations of 10% per year. Margin compression and a pattern of steady but subdued growth are affecting sentiment around the results, despite below-fair-value share pricing and a price-to-earnings ratio of 12.4x compared to the industry’s 16.7x average.

See our full analysis for AutoNation.

The next section examines how these headline numbers compare to the most widely followed narratives about AutoNation, revealing where expectations align or diverge.

After-Sales Delivers Record Revenue, Margins Hold Steady

  • After-sales operations, including service, parts, and collision work, drove record revenue and higher gross margins. This segment stands out as a key source of stable, high-margin income supporting overall company earnings.
  • Analysts' consensus view highlights that this growth is anchored in long-term trends, such as a rising average vehicle age and the expanding U.S. car parc. These factors are expected to underpin recurring, resilient revenue and earnings stability.
    • The company’s ongoing expansion of stand-alone used vehicle stores and continued densification in key markets builds on the broader industry trend of new vehicle under-supply since the pandemic.
    • Strategic investments in digital transformation, including improved data analytics and omnichannel sales, are credited with driving higher efficiency and broadening the customer base, in line with shifting preferences for online research and purchasing.
  • The consensus narrative notes that these factors position AutoNation to leverage secular trends in U.S. auto retail and strengthen earnings despite a slower forecasted growth rate versus the wider market. 📊 Read the full AutoNation Consensus Narrative.

Profit Margin Resilience Amid Shifting Industry Landscape

  • Analysts project that net profit margins will rise from 2.3% today to 2.9% over the next three years, suggesting a rebound in profitability even as growth lags behind broader market trends.
  • According to the consensus narrative, this margin resilience is set against a backdrop of growing risks:
    • Competitive pressures from direct-to-consumer and digital-first models like Carvana and Tesla could compress margins, as industry dynamics shift away from traditional dealerships.
    • The rise of electric vehicles threatens the long-term strength of high-margin aftersales services. Nearly half of AutoNation’s gross profit comes from these services, and EVs typically require less maintenance.

Valuation Below Fair Value but Growth Trails Sector

    • The narrative also points out a decline in shares outstanding by 4.89% per year, which could provide some support to earnings per share but isn’t expected to transform the overall growth outlook.

data.title: How Should Investors Value Brookfield Asset Management After Recent 11% Share Price Drop?
data.description:
Trying to decide whether to hold, buy, or sell Brookfield Asset Management stock? You are not alone. With a current share price of $75.2, this well-known asset manager has caught the attention of investors following both its recent volatility and shifting sector sentiment. Over the last seven days, shares are down 1.1%, and the slide over the past month is even more pronounced at -11.2%. Yet, looking past the short-term noise, Brookfield has managed a respectable 6.5% gain over the past year. These moves come in the context of a broader shift in the alternative asset management industry, as many players adjust strategies to navigate market uncertainty and shifting capital flows.

One factor weighing on the price appears to be cautious investor sentiment after a string of high-profile, sector-wide regulatory updates. While Brookfield itself has not been directly affected, the broader environment has prompted many to reassess risk, making valuation an even hotter topic in recent weeks.

Based on six common checks for undervaluation, Brookfield scores a 2, indicating it is undervalued in just 2 out of 6 areas. This suggests there may be some hidden value, but also some fair pricing factored in. So, how does this all stack up in terms of valuation? Here is a breakdown of the key valuation approaches used by analysts, followed by a more holistic perspective that could provide greater insight into Brookfield’s real worth.

Brookfield Asset Management scores just 2/6 on our valuation checks. See what other red flags we found in the full valuation breakdown.

Advertisement

Approach 1: Brookfield Asset Management Excess Returns Analysis

The Excess Returns model evaluates a company by looking at how much profit it generates over and above the cost of its equity capital. It focuses on the returns Brookfield Asset Management earns compared to what shareholders require, offering a picture of value based on sustainable profitability, not just headline earnings.

For Brookfield, the analysis shows a Book Value of CA$5.25 per share, with a projected Stable EPS of CA$1.96 per share. These estimates are based on the weighted future Return on Equity projections from four analysts. Brookfield’s average Return on Equity stands at an impressive 33.82%, while the Cost of Equity is CA$0.44 per share. The key number here is the Excess Return, at CA$1.52 per share, which reflects profits generated above the company's cost of capital. Projected Stable Book Value is CA$5.78 per share, again supported by analyst estimates.

The resulting intrinsic value from this model is CA$50.84 per share. Compared to the current share price of CA$75.20, the Excess Returns model indicates the stock is roughly 47.9% overvalued. That is a considerable premium over what the underlying profitability seems to justify at this time.

Result: OVERVALUED

Approach 2: Brookfield Asset Management Price vs Earnings

The Price-to-Earnings (PE) ratio is often the go-to metric for evaluating profitable companies like Brookfield Asset Management. It tells investors how much they are paying for each dollar of earnings, making it an especially relevant measure when a company has consistent profits and earnings growth.

However, what counts as a “normal” or “fair” PE ratio depends heavily on broader context. High-growth companies or those operating in lower-risk industries typically justify a higher PE, while mature, slow-growth, or riskier firms usually trade at lower multiples.

Brookfield currently trades at a PE ratio of 35.6x, well above the Capital Markets industry average of 8.7x and also below the average of its peers at 55.2x. To offer more rigorous perspective, the Simply Wall St “Fair Ratio” model forecasts a fair PE of 37.7x for Brookfield, based on signals such as its earnings outlook, profitability, market cap, and sector risks.

Unlike simple peer comparisons or industry averages, the Fair Ratio draws on a range of company-specific factors to gauge where the multiple should be. This means it adjusts for both the unique risks and strengths of Brookfield’s business, delivering a more nuanced estimate of fair value.

Comparing Brookfield’s actual PE (35.6x) to its Fair Ratio (37.7x), the difference is small, indicating the current price is about right based on this methodology.

Result: ABOUT RIGHT

Earlier, we mentioned there is an even better way to understand valuation, so let’s introduce you to Narratives. A Narrative is a simple yet powerful approach where you outline your story or perspective on a company, linking this story directly to your financial forecasts and assumptions, such as estimates of fair value, future revenues, and profit margins. data.title: Is Now the Right Time to Reassess Twilio After Its Recent 55.6% One Year Rally?
data.description:
If you have been tracking Twilio lately, you know the stock has kept everyone guessing. Maybe you are wondering if now is the time to double down or finally take some profits off the table. After all, Twilio’s share price recently closed at $110.82, up an impressive 4.3% just in the past week and 7.1% over the last month. The yearly return is even more eye-catching at 55.6%. However, longer-term investors are still nursing wounds from a 61.6% drop over five years.

So what has sparked this recent momentum? Analysts and investors have noticed that Twilio's evolving strategy, especially as the company sharpens its focus on customer engagement and retools its product roadmap, has led to a renewed sense of optimism. News of fresh partnerships and a streamlined cost structure have also helped shift risk perceptions, fueling short-term gains. While there are still concerns about profitable growth, the market seems to be warming up to Twilio’s prospects again.

With all these moving parts, valuation has become the hot topic. Twilio currently sports a value score of 3 out of 6, meaning it ranks as undervalued in half of the key valuation checks used by analysts. But what exactly goes into that score, and does it tell the whole story? Up next, I will break down these different valuation approaches, showing how each one paints a unique picture of Twilio’s worth. And stick around, because I will share a smarter way to interpret all this data before we wrap up.

Twilio delivered 55.6% returns over the last year. See how this stacks up to the rest of the IT industry.

Advertisement

Approach 1: Twilio Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) model estimates a company’s value by projecting all future cash flows and then discounting them back to today's dollars. This provides a present value of the business and gives investors a way to judge whether a stock is trading above or below its calculated intrinsic worth.

For Twilio, the DCF model uses a “2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity” approach. In the last twelve months, the company generated $714.3 million in free cash flow. Analyst estimates chart this growing steadily, with projections reaching $1,007.4 million by 2026 and $1,264.2 million by 2029. Beyond that, further growth is projected using conservative assumptions, bringing free cash flow estimates to approximately $1.62 billion by 2035. All figures are in US dollars.

The calculation results in an intrinsic value of about $132.15 per share, which sits 16.1% above Twilio’s recent closing price. This suggests the market might be undervaluing Twilio relative to its long-term cash generation potential, based on these forecasts and assumptions.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for Twilio.

Our Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) analysis suggests Twilio is undervalued by 16.1%. Track this in your watchlist or portfolio, or discover more undervalued stocks.

Approach 2: Twilio Price vs Sales

The price-to-sales (P/S) ratio is a popular valuation metric for companies like Twilio, where profitability may not yet be consistent but revenue growth is strong and central to the business model. By measuring how much investors are willing to pay for each dollar of sales, the P/S ratio helps you compare companies regardless of their profitability. This makes it especially useful for high-growth technology firms.

Twilio is currently trading at a P/S ratio of 3.60x. For context, the average P/S across the IT industry is 2.78x, while the average P/S among Twilio’s listed peers is significantly higher at 6.69x. A P/S ratio above the industry average can reflect higher growth expectations or lower perceived risk, but it can also signal heightened risk if not supported by fundamentals.

This is where Simply Wall St’s Fair Ratio is relevant. Unlike a simple comparison against peers or the industry, the Fair Ratio considers Twilio’s earnings growth potential, industry positioning, profit margins, market capitalization and specific risk factors. It sets an expectation for a fair multiple by factoring in much more than headline growth. Right now, Twilio’s Fair Ratio is 4.67x compared to its actual 3.60x P/S, suggesting the stock is priced below where it could reasonably trade given its outlook and risk profile.
